name: Tin Mountain
short_description: High-elevation Harney Peak granite terrain near Custer offering quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Gentle slopes and scenic Black Hills setting make this a rewarding spot for intermediate rockho...
description:
Tin Mountain sits in the heart of the Black Hills at 5,736 feet elevation, where the underlying Harney Peak granite formation provides excellent mineral collecting opportunities. The area's gentle slopes and accessible terrain make it suitable for rockhounds of moderate skill. The mountain's name reflects historical prospecting interest in the region, though today collectors focus on quality mineral specimens rather than tin extraction.

The Harney Peak granite here contains visible quartz crystals, feldspar, and mica in various formations. Collectors often find well-formed specimens on hillsides where weathering has exposed fresh crystal faces. The gentle slope terrain allows for extended exploring without excessive scrambling, and the relative elevation keeps the area cooler during warmer months.

This location rewards patient searching along outcrops and in weathered granite debris fields. The combination of quality specimens and manageable access makes Tin Mountain a solid destination for building a Black Hills mineral collection. Bring water and sun protection despite the elevation, as the open terrain offers little shade.
history: Tin Mountain sits within the Harney Peak granite pluton, a massive 1.7-billion-year-old Precambrian granite formation that makes up much of the Black Hills core. Historical prospectors in the late 1800s explored this region for tin and other minerals, though the area never became a major mining center. The granite's coarse-grained composition and abundant feldspar and quartz crystals make it an ideal hunting ground for contemporary rockhounds.
tips: Search the weathered granite outcrops on the gentle south and west-facing slopes where water runoff and freeze-thaw cycles have exposed fresh crystal faces. Use a hand lens to identify feldspar twinning patterns and quartz terminations before digging; this saves time and helps you target the best specimens. Look for mica layers in the granite - they often peel away naturally and reveal nice quartz crystals underneath. Bring a small rock hammer and chisel, but work carefully on the granite to avoid shattering specimens.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,736 feet with gentle rolling slopes and scattered granite outcrops. Sparse Black Hills vegetation including ponderosa pine and rocky ground cover. Walking distance from parking typically 0.25 to 1 mile depending on where outcrops are accessed. Footing is moderately rocky with loose granite fragments and stable soil between outcrops. No significant elevation gain once on site.
best_season: June - September. High elevation means snow blocks access October through May. Summer provides reliable dry conditions and clear visibility, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible. Early morning starts recommended to avoid afternoon storms and intense midday heat at this elevation.
written_directions: From Custer, take South Dakota Highway 89 north approximately 8 miles toward Hill City. Look for Forest Service road markers or mountain access signs on the west side of the highway in the vicinity of Tin Mountain's mapped location. Turn west onto the appropriate access road (conditions vary seasonally) and proceed 1-2 miles to parking areas near exposed granite outcrops. GPS coordinates 43.755743, -103.720087 mark the primary collecting area. High-clearance vehicle recommended during wet seasons.
road_conditions: Forest Service road, seasonal washboard, high-clearance recommended when wet
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas near granite outcrops. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No developed facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Remove only loose material and specimens that can be safely extracted without explosives or heavy machinery. Leave site as you found it. National Forest regulations apply.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No explosives or heavy machinery
Leave no trace - pack out all waste
Respect adjacent private property
nearest_services: Custer, South Dakota, approximately 8 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky ground and granite outcrops. Sturdy hiking boots required. Some scrambling over loose rocks to reach best specimen areas. Not suitable for wheelchairs or limited mobility. Elevation of 5,736 feet may affect those sensitive to altitude. Plan for 2-4 hours of collecting activity.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Tin Mountain in South Dakota?
faq.answer: Tin Mountain is best known for high-quality quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, mica, and garnet found within the Harney Peak granite formation. Quartz crystals often display excellent terminations and clarity, while feldspar shows characteristic twinning patterns. The granite's coarse grain size makes these minerals easy to spot and extract without damage.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Tin Mountain free or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Tin Mountain is free and does not require a permit. The area sits on National Forest land managed for public use. You must follow FLPMA regulations: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time to go rockhounding at Tin Mountain?
faq.answer: June through September is the best season. The high elevation (5,736 feet) means heavy snow blocks access October through May. Summer provides dry conditions, clear visibility, and stable access roads, though afternoon thunderstorms can develop. Early morning visits are recommended to avoid storms and intense midday heat.

faq.question: Is Tin Mountain rockhounding suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Tin Mountain is moderate difficulty and better suited for rockhounds with some experience. The gentle terrain is accessible, but you need rock identification skills to spot quality specimens among common granite fragments. Beginners should start with easier Black Hills locations like French Creek or study granite mineral formation before visiting. Kids ages 8+ with adult supervision can collect here successfully.

faq.question: What tools do you need to collect minerals at Tin Mountain?
faq.answer: A rock hammer and small chisel work best for carefully extracting specimens from granite outcrops. Bring a hand lens to identify crystals before collecting. Sturdy boots with good grip are essential on the rocky slopes. Pack water, sun protection, and a collection bag. A headlamp is useful for early-morning trips to avoid afternoon storms.

faq.question: How far is Tin Mountain from Custer, South Dakota?
faq.answer: Tin Mountain is approximately 8 miles north of Custer via South Dakota Highway 89. The drive takes about 15 minutes. Custer offers full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies. The remote location requires high-clearance vehicles, especially during wet seasons on Forest Service access roads.

meta_title: Tin Mountain Rockhounding - South Dakota Granite
